product direction / onboarding

Onboarding direction for a privacy-first budgeting product

A decision brief for product and design leads: choose the first-run path that makes import consequences legible while preserving household control.

On this page

Decision Decision criteria Options Observed evidence First-run path Limitations Next validation
On this page
Decision Decision criteria Options Observed evidence First-run path Limitations Next validation

Choose statement-first import

Decision. Start with statement-first import: validate the file, show a grouped preview, then require explicit apply. Offer a short, optional interview about household goals after the first successful import.

4 / 5
asked what would change before importing
moderated sessions
3 / 5
abandoned guided interview at recurring transfers
moderated sessions
5 / 5
understood the grouped statement preview
Added · Matched · Needs review
none
production funnel data
not available yet

The observed pattern favors a fast route to useful data that makes the privacy boundary legible before anything changes. The interview remains useful, but the first-run gate is too costly when it asks people to classify recurring transfers.

What the onboarding choice must balance

For a privacy-first household budgeting product, the first run needs to make the consequence of importing understandable, reach useful data quickly, and leave room for user control. These are the comparison lenses used below.

Boundary. The notes contain moderated-session evidence only. There is no production funnel data yet, so this is a directional product decision, not a measured conversion claim.

Three viable onboarding directions

Guided interview

Strongest explanation and trust, but a 6–8 minute path with the highest completion burden. Three of five moderated participants abandoned when asked to classify recurring transfers.

best for: household goals and context after value is visible

Statement-first import

Recommended. Fastest path to useful data at 2–3 minutes. It earns trust when the preview groups changed rows as Added, Matched, and Needs review before an explicit apply.

best for: a privacy-first first run that shows consequences before commit

Empty workspace

Maximum agency and no upfront commitment, but the lowest initial comprehension. People must discover both import and categories without a guided starting point.

best for: users who already know how they want to set up the workspace

The key difference is not whether users can import; it is whether the first action explains what will change before the product asks for commitment.

Observed evidence points to preview before apply

The focal figure keeps the three observed signals together. All counts come from the same five moderated sessions; they are observations, not a production funnel.

Observed signalCountWhat it means for onboarding
Asked what would change before importing 4 of 5 Make the consequence of import visible before commit.
Abandoned guided interview at recurring transfers 3 of 5 Move high-effort classification out of the first gate.
Understood Added / Matched / Needs review preview 5 of 5 Keep grouped preview as the trust and control checkpoint.

Proposed first-run path

Keep the initial promise narrow: show the household what the import will do, then return agency before applying anything.

  1. 1
    Validate

    Check the statement before presenting changes.

  2. 2
    Preview

    Group changed rows as Added, Matched, and Needs review.

  3. 3
    Explicit apply

    Let the household commit only after reviewing the preview.

  4. 4
    Optional interview

    Ask about household goals after the first successful import.

This sequence preserves the interview's trust-building purpose without making recurring-transfer classification a prerequisite for first value.

Confidence and limitations

Small sample. The evidence is from five moderated sessions. It is useful for identifying comprehension and friction signals, but it cannot estimate completion or retention at scale.

Missing production data. No production funnel data exists yet. Instrument import start, preview reach, explicit apply, and optional-interview uptake before treating this direction as quantitatively validated.

Next validation after launch

Ship the contained first-run path, then use the production funnel to test whether the observed comprehension advantage translates into completed imports and sustained use.

Choose statement-first import. Make validation, grouped preview, and explicit apply the first-run contract. Keep the short interview optional and place it after the household has seen useful data.

Before calling the decision proven, measure the path from import start to preview, explicit apply, and optional interview uptake. The current notes support the direction; they do not supply production conversion rates.
